title: The Power of Pretraining
concept: Unsupervised pretraining on raw web text acts as a universal feature extractor, reducing the amount of labeled data needed for any task by 90%.

exercises.prompt: What is the term for training an AI on general web text before teaching it specific tasks?
exercises.explain: Pretraining uses raw web text to learn general features of language before the model is tuned for specific tasks.

cards.title: The Shortcut to Smart AI
cards.body: Imagine you had to learn a brand-new language from scratch just to read a single recipe. Sounds exhausting, right? That is what AI used to do for every single task. Today, AI gets to read the entire internet first to understand how language works, making all future tasks a breeze!

cards.title: What is Pretraining?
cards.body: Pretraining is the phase where an AI reads massive amounts of raw web text without any human labels. During this, it acts as a 'universal feature extractor'—learning grammar, logic, and facts. Because it already understands the world, it needs 90% less labeled data to learn a specific new task.

cards.title: The Bilingual Toddler
cards.body: Think of pretraining like teaching a child how to read, speak, and understand the general structure of language before asking them to write a specific book report. Because they already know how language works, you only need to show them a few examples of a book report for them to get it right. Without that basic training, you'd have to teach them the alphabet first!

cards.title: From Internet to Medicine
cards.body: Instead of training an AI from scratch to read medical reports (which would require doctors to hand-label millions of pages), engineers take a model pretrained on the whole internet. Because it already understands English, it only needs a few hundred labeled medical reports to become an expert assistant.

cards.title: The 'Start From Scratch' Myth
cards.body: Some believe that to teach an AI a new task, you must train it from scratch using only data for that specific task. In reality, starting from scratch requires huge datasets. Pretrained models already have a massive head start, allowing them to master new tasks with tiny amounts of data.

cards.title: The Data Shortcut
cards.body: Look at how much human effort is saved when we switch from training a model from scratch to using a pretrained model.

left.text: Needs millions of custom, hand-labeled examples for every single task.

right.text: Needs 90% less labeled data because the model already understands language.

exercises.prompt: What type of data does an AI use during unsupervised pretraining?
exercises.explain: Raw internet text does not have labels like 'spam' or 'not spam' attached to it. The AI learns just by reading the patterns of the text itself.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Pretraining on raw web text decreases the amount of labeled data you need for specific tasks later on.
exercises.explain: Pretraining helps the AI learn general language features, which cuts down the amount of labeled task data needed by 90%.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing reduction percentage that pretraining provides for labeled data.
exercises.sentence: Pretraining on raw web text acts as a universal feature extractor, reducing the amount of labeled data needed for any task by [blank].
exercises.explain: Because the AI learns grammar, reasoning, and facts during pretraining, it requires 90% less labeled data to learn a new task.

exercises.prompt: Which AI assistant proposes the most efficient way to build a legal document classifier?

options.text: AI A: 'Since you want to classify legal documents, we must gather millions of legal contracts and train a brand new model from scratch.'
AI B: 'Let's take a model pretrained on the web, and show it just a few hundred legal documents to teach it what to look for.'
AI C: 'We should hire thousands of people to label the entire internet before training our legal AI model.'

exercises.explain: AI B is correct because unsupervised pretraining allows the model to act as a feature extractor, requiring only a tiny amount of target-specific data to adapt. AI A describes the old, inefficient way of training from scratch.

exercises.prompt: You are explaining the power of pretraining to a tech investor. How does pretraining successfully reduce the amount of labeled data needed for a new task by 90%?

options.text: Pretraining teaches the AI general language features first, so it needs far less specific training later.
Pretraining generates artificial labeled data so humans don't have to write any.
Pretraining deletes unnecessary parts of the model to make it run faster.
Pretraining requires humans to label billions of web pages by hand.

exercises.explain: Pretraining acts as a universal feature extractor by learning the general structure of language first. This massive starting knowledge means you only need about 10% of the usual labeled data to teach the AI a specific new task.
